sources.citation: Bloom et al. Taxonomy 1956; Anderson & Krathwohl 2001.
Roediger & Karpicke. Test-Enhanced Learning. Psych Science, 2006.
U.S. Dept. of Education. Evidence-Based Practices in Online Learning. 2010.

learning_objectives: L1: State the six levels of Bloom's Revised Taxonomy and their associated cognitive verbs.
L1: Define spaced repetition, retrieval practice, and interleaving.
L1: Identify intrinsic, extraneous, and germane cognitive load.
L2: Explain why retrieval practice produces stronger retention than re-reading.
L2: Describe the forgetting curve and how spaced repetition counters it.
L2: Compare formative and summative assessment.
L3: Design a five-question mini-quiz covering Bloom's L1, L2, and L3 for a given topic.
L3: Apply cognitive load theory to evaluate an instructional design.

concepts.title: The Six Levels
concepts.explanation: L1 Remember: define, identify, list, state. L2 Understand: explain, describe, classify, compare. L3 Apply: implement, execute, solve, compute. L4 Analyse: differentiate, examine. L5 Evaluate: critique, justify, assess. L6 Create: design, construct, develop. Mini-quizzes target L1–L3.
concepts.worked_example: Binary Search — L1: State time complexity. L2: Explain why a sorted array is required. L3: Trace binary search on [2,5,8,12,16,23] for target=12.

concepts.title: Forgetting Curve and Spaced Repetition
concepts.explanation: Ebbinghaus (1885): memory decays exponentially — 1 day ~33% retained, 1 week ~25%. Each successful retrieval resets curve with shallower slope. Spaced repetition: review at 1, 3, 7, 14, 30 days.
concepts.worked_example: Cramming 5h: 70% next day, 20% after 2 weeks. Spaced 1h×5: 60% next day, 65% after 2 weeks — ~3× better long-term.

concepts.title: The Testing Effect
concepts.explanation: Retrieval practice produces stronger learning than re-reading. Roediger & Karpicke (2006): study-test group scored ~50% better at 1-week test. Retrieval is effortful — reconstructing a memory strengthens the encoding.
concepts.worked_example: This platform: each mini-lesson followed by 5-question mini-quiz (immediate retrieval). SuperQuizzes revisit earlier material (spaced retrieval).

concepts.title: Three Types of Load
concepts.explanation: Sweller (1988): working memory has limited capacity. Intrinsic: inherent difficulty — manage via simple-to-complex sequencing. Extraneous: poor design overhead — minimise. Germane: schema-building effort — maximise.
concepts.worked_example: High extraneous: 200-word preamble, three tables, scrolling required. Low extraneous: concise stem under 50 words, single diagram, four choices visible without scrolling.

mini_lessons.title: Assessment Design and AI in Education

concepts.title: Formative vs Summative
concepts.explanation: Formative: ongoing, low-stakes feedback — diagnose gaps, guide instruction. Summative: evaluates learning at end of unit. Formative = assessment FOR learning; summative = assessment OF learning.
concepts.worked_example: Student fails MQ-3 Q4. Platform shows correct answer + sourced explanation. SuperQuiz B revisits same concept later — spaced retrieval reinforces corrected understanding.

concepts.title: AI in Education
concepts.explanation: Four applications: (1) Adaptive learning. (2) Intelligent tutoring (Bloom 2-sigma). (3) Automated essay scoring. (4) Learning analytics. Equity concerns: performance gaps, surveillance, unequal access.
concepts.worked_example: Bloom's 2-sigma: 1-on-1 tutored students score 2 SD above classroom-taught peers. Source: Educational Researcher 13(6). Free: https://www.jstor.org/stable/1175554
